trap: 🚨 Using the wrong characteristic length for the particle shape (e.g., radius instead of radius/3 for a sphere) gives an incorrect Thiele modulus and effectiveness factor.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Wagner-Weisz-Wheeler Modulus
desc: The Wagner modulus M_W = M_T² η = L² (-r_A,obs / C_As) / 𝒟ₑ is an observable quantity. It allows determination of whether pore diffusion is significant without knowing the intrinsic kinetics. For first‑order reactions, M_W = (observed rate) × L² / (𝒟ₑ C_As). M_W < 0.15 indicates negligible pore resistance; M_W > 4 indicates strong pore resistance.
formula: M_W = L² (-r_A,obs) / (𝒟ₑ C_As) = M_T² η.
trap: 🚌 Using M_W to compare different particle sizes assumes the reaction order is known and that the intrinsic rate is first‑order; for other orders, the relationship is approximate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Effect of Strong Pore Diffusion on Kinetics
desc: Under strong pore diffusion (M_T > 4), the observed reaction order becomes (n+1)/2, and the observed activation energy becomes approximately half the true activation energy (since diffusion activation energy is small). For multiple reactions, the observed rate constant ratio (k₂/k₁) becomes (k₂/k₁)^(1/2) for parallel reactions, and (k₂/k₁)^(1/2) also affects intermediate yield in series reactions, reducing it.
formula: n_obs = (n+1)/2; E_obs ≈ E_true / 2; (k₂/k₁)_obs = (k₂/k₁)^(1/2).
trap: 🚨 Interpreting experimental activation energy as the true activation energy when pore diffusion is significant leads to underestimation of the true activation energy.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance Equations for Catalytic Reactors
desc: For plug flow: W/F_A0 = ∫ dX_A/(-r_A'), where -r_A' is the rate per mass of catalyst. For mixed flow: W/F_A0 = (X_Aout - X_Ain)/(-r_A,out'). For batch gas with catalyst: t/(C_A0) = (V/W) ∫ dX_A/(-r_A'). These are analogous to homogeneous reactor equations but with weight instead of volume.
formula: Plug flow: W/F_A0 = ∫₀^{X_Af} dX_A/(-r_A'); mixed flow: W/F_A0 = X_Af/(-r_Af'); batch: t = (W/V) ∫₀^{X_A} dX_A/(-r_A').
trap: 🚨 Using volume‑based rates (per m³ reactor) when the catalyst weight‑based rate is available leads to inconsistency; always match the basis.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Experimental Reactors for Catalytic Kinetics
desc: Common experimental devices: differential reactor (small conversion, rate directly from ΔX/Δ(W/F_A0)), integral reactor (plug flow, used with integrated rate equations), mixed flow (basket or recycle, rate from exit conditions), and batch gas‑solid reactor (both phases batch, composition vs time). Each has advantages: differential and mixed flow give rates directly; integral reactor models large units; batch is flexible for multiple reactions.
trap: 🚨 Using an integral reactor with a differential analysis when conversion is large can cause errors if the rate is not averaged properly; differential reactors require small conversion (<10%).

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Langmuir-Hinshelwood Rate Equations
desc: Rate expressions derived from mechanistic surface kinetics (adsorption, reaction, desorption) have the form: rate = (kinetic term)(driving force) / (resistance term)². For a reaction A + B ⇌ R + S, the rate can have many forms depending on which step controls. Despite their complexity, most catalytic data can be well fitted by simple power‑law kinetics, making them practical for design.
formula: For dual‑site reaction control: -r_A'' = [k (p_A p_B - p_R p_S / K)] / (1 + K_A p_A + K_B p_B + K_R p_R + K_S p_S)².
trap: 🚨 Overfitting with a complex Langmuir‑Hinshelwood model when a simple power law fits equally well does not necessarily prove the mechanism; it is often impossible to discriminate among mechanisms with typical experimental error.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Nonisothermal Effects in Catalyst Particles
desc: For exothermic reactions, the catalyst particle can be hotter than the bulk gas due to heat release. The film temperature gradient ΔT_film ≈ L (-r_A,obs) (-ΔH_r)/h. The intraparticle gradient ΔT_particle ≈ 𝒟ₑ (C_As) (-ΔH_r)/k_eff. For gases, film gradients usually appear first; intraparticle gradients are small unless the reaction is very fast. Nonisothermal effectiveness factors can exceed 1 for exothermic reactions because the temperature rise accelerates the rate.
formula: ΔT_film = L (-r_A,obs) (-ΔH_r)/h; ΔT_particle = 𝒟ₑ (C_As) (-ΔH_r)/k_eff.
trap: 🚨 Ignoring temperature gradients when they are significant can cause severe errors in rate predictions; the observed activation energy may be lower than true due to combined diffusion and heat effects.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Determining Controlling Resistances (Example 18.1)
problem: A first‑order reaction is run on spherical catalyst particles (d_p=2.4 mm, L=0.4 mm, 𝒟ₑ=5×10⁻⁵ m³/(hr·m_cat), k_eff=1.6 kJ/(hr·m_cat·K), h=160 kJ/(hr·m²·K), k_g=300 m³/(hr·m²), ΔH_r=-160 kJ/mol, C_Ag=20 mol/m³, -r_A,obs=10⁵ mol/(hr·m³_cat). Determine if film mass transfer, pore diffusion, or nonisothermal effects are significant.
solution_steps: Step 1: Check film mass transfer. Compare observed rate to film transfer rate: (observed rate)/(film-limited rate) = (-r_A,obs/C_Ag) * (d_p/6) / k_g = (10⁵/20) * (0.0024/6) / 300 = 0.0067 << 1, so film mass transfer negligible.
Step 2: Check pore diffusion using Wagner modulus: M_W = L² (-r_A,obs) / (𝒟ₑ C_Ag) = (0.0004)² × 10⁵ / (5×10⁻⁵ × 20) = 16. Since M_W > 4, strong pore diffusion resistance is present.

Step 3: Check nonisothermal effects: max intraparticle ΔT = 𝒟ₑ C_Ag (-ΔH_r) / k_eff = (5×10⁻⁵ × 20 × 160) / 1.6 = 0.1°C (negligible). Max film ΔT = L (-r_A,obs) (-ΔH_r) / h = (0.0004 × 10⁵ × 160) / 160 = 40°C (significant). So particle is hotter than gas but uniform inside.
formula_used: M_W = L² (-r_A,obs)/(𝒟ₑ C_Ag); ΔT_film = L (-r_A,obs)(-ΔH_r)/h; ΔT_particle = 𝒟ₑ C_Ag (-ΔH_r)/k_eff.
final_answer: Film mass transfer negligible; strong pore diffusion present; film temperature gradient significant (40°C), intraparticle gradient negligible.
trap: 🚨 The observed rate being much lower than the film transfer rate does not guarantee no pore diffusion; the Wagner modulus test is needed to assess pore resistance.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Rate Equation from Differential Reactor (Example 18.2)
problem: Catalytic reaction A → 4R is run in a plug flow reactor with 0.01 kg catalyst, feed of 20 L/hr pure A at 3.2 atm and 117°C (C_A0=0.1 mol/L). Exit concentrations: run1:0.084, run2:0.070, run3:0.055, run4:0.038 mol/L. Find rate equation.
solution_steps: Step 1: Because conversion is small (max ΔX_A ≈ 0.045), treat as differential reactor. For each run, average concentration C_Av = (C_Ain + C_Aout)/2. Compute X_Ain, X_Aout using variable‑density relation with ε_A=3: X_A = (1 - C_A/0.1)/(1+3 C_A/0.1).
Step 2: Calculate ΔX_A = X_Aout - X_Ain, then -r_A' = (ΔX_A) / (W/F_A0) with F_A0=2 mol/hr, W=0.01 kg.
Step 3: Plot -r_A' vs C_Av. The points fall on a straight line through origin, indicating first‑order. Slope = 96 L/(hr·kg_cat).
Step 4: Rate equation: -r_A' = 96 C_A (mol/(kg_cat·hr)), C_A in mol/L.
trap: 🚨 For gas‑phase reactions with mole change, using constant‑density conversion formula X_A = 1 - C_A/C_A0 would give wrong X_A values; the variable‑density relation must be used.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Rate Equation from Integral Reactor (Example 18.3)
problem: Same reaction as Example 18.2 but with larger conversions (up to 38%). Data: W (kg): 0.02, 0.04, 0.08, 0.16; corresponding C_Aout (mol/L): 0.074, 0.060, 0.044, 0.029. Find rate equation by (a) integral method, (b) differential method.
solution_steps: Step 1 (a) Integral method: For first‑order plug flow, (1+ε_A)ln(1/(1-X_A)) - ε_A X_A = (k' C_A0 W)/F_A0. Compute X_A from C_Aout using ε_A=3, then left side. Plot left side vs W. Straight line through origin confirms first‑order; slope = k' C_A0 / F_A0 = 0.00475 kg⁻¹, so k' = 0.00475 × (2/0.1) = 95 L/(hr·kg_cat).

Step 2 (b) Differential method: Plot X_A vs W/F_A0, draw smooth curve, take slopes at several points. Slopes give -r_A' = dX_A/d(W/F_A0). Compute corresponding C_A from X_A using C_A = C_A0 (1-X_A)/(1+ε_A X_A). Plot -r_A' vs C_A; straight line through origin gives k' = 93 L/(hr·kg_cat).
formula_used: Plug flow performance: (1+ε_A)ln(1/(1-X_A)) - ε_A X_A = k' C_A0 W/F_A0.
trap: 🚨 In integral analysis, using the wrong integrated form (e.g., constant‑density) for a variable‑density reaction leads to erroneous k'.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Plug Flow Reactor Size from Rate Equation (Example 18.4)
problem: Using the rate equation -r_A' = 96 C_A (from Example 18.2), find the catalyst weight needed in a PFR for 35% conversion of A, feed 2000 mol/hr pure A at 3.2 atm and 117°C (C_A0=0.1 mol/L, ε_A=3).
solution_steps: Step 1: Use plug flow performance equation: W/F_A0 = (1/(k' C_A0))[(1+ε_A)ln(1/(1-X_A)) - ε_A X_A].
Step 2: Substitute: F_A0=2000 mol/hr, k'=96 L/(hr·kg), C_A0=0.1 mol/L, ε_A=3, X_A=0.35.
trap: 🚨 Forgetting the expansion factor ε_A in the integrated equation would give W = (F_A0/(k' C_A0)) ln(1/(1-X_A)) = 2000×0.43078/(9.6) ≈ 90 kg, which underestimates the required catalyst.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Plug Flow Reactor Size from Rate Data (Example 18.5)
problem: Given rate‑concentration data for the same reaction (r_A' vs C_A), find catalyst weight for 35% conversion without deriving a rate equation. Data points: -r_A' = 3.4, 5.4, 7.6, 9.1 at C_A = 0.039, 0.0575, 0.075, 0.092 (units consistent). Use graphical integration.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute X_A for each C_A using X_A = (1 - C_A/0.1)/(1+3 C_A/0.1). Values: 0.281, 0.156, 0.0778, 0.0227.
Step 3: Plot 1/(-r_A') vs X_A and integrate from 0 to 0.35. Area ≈ 0.0735.
formula_used: W = F_A0 ∫₀^{X_Af} dX_A/(-r_A').
trap: 🚨 Extrapolating the rate data beyond the measured range can introduce error; the graphical integration must be done carefully.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Mixed Flow Reactor Size (Example 18.6)
problem: For the same reaction, find catalyst weight needed in a mixed flow reactor for 35% conversion, using rate equation -r_A' = 96 C_A, C_A0=0.1, ε_A=3, F_A0=2000 mol/hr.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute exit concentration at X_A=0.35: C_A = C_A0 (1-X_A)/(1+ε_A X_A) = 0.1 × (0.65)/(1+1.05) = 0.1 × 0.65/2.05 = 0.0317 mol/L.
Step 2: Rate at exit: -r_A' = 96 × 0.0317 = 3.043 mol/(kg·hr).
Step 3: Mixed flow equation: W = F_A0 X_A / (-r_A') = 2000 × 0.35 / 3.043 = 230 kg.
formula_used: W = F_A0 X_A / (-r_A') for CSTR.
trap: 🚨 Using the inlet concentration in the rate would give a much smaller weight, but the rate must be evaluated at exit conditions.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Interpreting Rate Data for Controlling Resistance (Example 18.7)
problem: Kinetic data from a basket mixed flow reactor with different pellet diameters and spinning rates: (1) small pellets, high spin, rate=1; (2) large pellets, high spin, rate=1; (3) large pellets, low spin, rate=1. All at same conditions. Determine controlling resistances.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compare runs 2 and 3 (large pellets, different spin rates). Same rate → film resistance not controlling (if film were controlling, rate would change with spin).

Step 2: Compare run 1 (small pellets) with run 2 (large pellets). Both at high spin, same conditions. Rate same for different pellet sizes → indicates strong pore diffusion resistance, because for strong pore diffusion, rate ∝ 1/L, so if sizes differ, rates would differ. But here they are equal? Actually the problem states: run1 (small) gives rate=1, run2 (large) gives rate=1. If strong pore diffusion, rate would be inversely proportional to particle size, so small pellets should give higher rate. Since rates are equal, likely both are in the strong pore diffusion regime but the pellet sizes are not given numerically? Wait, the problem statement in the text is a bit ambiguous. The table shows: run1: small pellets, high spin, rate=1; run2: large pellets, high spin, rate=1; run3: large pellets, low spin, rate=1. The interpretation in the text: Runs 2 and 3 use different spinning rates but have the same reaction rate. Therefore film diffusion is ruled out for the larger pellets. But Eq. 57 shows that if film resistance is not important for large pellets it will not be important for small pellets. Therefore film resistance does not influence the rate. Next, comparing run 1 and either run 2 or 3 shows that -r_A' ∝ 1/R? Actually they say: Comparing run 1 and either run 2 or 3 shows that -r_A' ∝ 1/R. But the rates are equal, so if sizes are different, the rate being equal implies that the rate is independent of size, which would happen if the reaction is in the diffusion‑free regime (η=1) or if there is compensation? Actually the example in the text concludes: Eq. 25 then tells us that we are in the regime of strong pore resistance. Wait, Eq. 25 says in strong pore resistance, r ∝ 1/R. If the rate is the same for different R, that would mean the product r*R is constant, but if they didn't give the actual sizes, they cannot conclude 1/R. Actually the example as presented in the text is likely intended to show that because the rate is the same for both sizes, and since in strong pore resistance the rate would be higher for smaller pellets, the fact that rates are equal suggests that the small pellets are also in strong pore resistance but perhaps the size difference is not large? The conclusion in the example is: negligible film resistance, strong pore diffusion resistance. We'll follow the text's reasoning: because spinning rate doesn't affect rate, film resistance is negligible; because rate is the same for different pellet sizes, pore diffusion is strong (the rate is limited by diffusion, and the characteristic length cancels out in the observed rate? Actually if both are in strong diffusion regime, the rate per unit mass is ∝ 1/L. If the sizes are different, the rates would be different unless the sizes are the same. So the statement that rates are equal for different sizes suggests that the pellets are of similar size? Or maybe the sizes were not actually different? The problem in the book likely uses the data to illustrate that since spinning rate doesn't change rate, film resistance is absent, and since the rate is low (and maybe the pellets are large), pore diffusion is likely. We'll present the logic as in the text.
final_answer: Negligible film resistance; strong pore diffusion resistance.
trap: 🚨 If film resistance were controlling, changing the spinning rate would change the rate. Since it does not, film resistance is absent. If pore diffusion were absent, smaller pellets would give higher rates; equal rates for different sizes (if sizes differ) would indicate strong diffusion control but with compensating factors? Actually the example in the text says: Comparing run 1 and either run 2 or 3 shows that -r_A' ∝ 1/R – which implies the rates are inversely proportional to size, meaning they are in strong pore diffusion regime. The numerical rates in the table are all 1, so the proportionality is not shown numerically. We'll stick to the conclusion given.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Fixed Bed vs Fluidized Bed Catalytic Reactors
desc: Fixed beds approximate plug flow, are simple, but suffer from poor heat transfer and require large particles to avoid pressure drop. Fluidized beds have excellent temperature control, can use small particles, and allow continuous catalyst regeneration, but have complex flow patterns with bypassing that reduce conversion and intermediate yields. Moving beds combine some features of both.
trap: 🚨 Assuming plug flow in a fluidized bed leads to overestimation of conversion and intermediate yields; real fluidized beds often have significant gas bypassing.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Staged Adiabatic Packed Beds with Intercooling
desc: For exothermic reversible reactions, staged adiabatic beds with intercooling can approximate the optimal temperature progression. The design procedure involves choosing an inlet temperature, determining the first‑stage exit where the rate is optimized (or using trial‑and‑error), cooling to a point with the same rate, and repeating. The total catalyst weight is minimized when the rate at the exit of one stage equals the rate at the inlet of the next after cooling.
formula: Criterion: (-r_A')_out, stage i = (-r_A')_in, stage i+1 (after cooling).
trap: 🚌 Cooling below the point where the rate matches the previous exit rate reduces the benefit of staging; the optimum interstage temperature is determined by equal rates, not by a fixed temperature difference.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Cold Shot Cooling in Packed Beds
desc: Cold shot cooling eliminates interstage heat exchangers by injecting cold feed directly into subsequent stages. It is practical when the feed temperature is much lower than the reaction temperature and when the temperature rise during reaction is large enough that the mixed temperature remains favorable. The criterion is T_reaction - T_feed > -ΔH_r/C_p.
formula: Useful when T_reaction - T_f > -ΔH_r / C_p.
trap: 🚨 Applying cold shot cooling when the feed temperature is already near the reaction temperature can cause the mixture to fall below the ignition point, stalling the reaction.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Choice of Contacting System for Exothermic Reactions
desc: For exothermic reactions, the slope of the adiabatic line (C_p/(-ΔH_r)) determines the best contacting pattern. Small slope (pure gas, large temperature rise) favors mixed flow (or high recycle) to avoid low‑rate regions. Large slope (dilute gas or liquid, small temperature rise) favors plug flow. Cold shot cooling is appropriate when preheating to reaction temperature is required but the adiabatic rise is not excessive.
formula: Slope = C_p / (-ΔH_r). Small slope → mixed flow; large slope → plug flow.
trap: 🚌 Using plug flow for a pure gas exothermic reaction with a shallow adiabatic line may result in a very large reactor volume because the rate remains low until high conversion; recycle or mixed flow can avoid the low‑rate region.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Design Procedure for Two-Stage Adiabatic Packed Beds
desc: For two stages with intercooling, the optimum is found by a one‑parameter search (inlet temperature). Steps: (1) Guess T_in; (2) follow adiabatic until the exit rate equals the rate at the inlet of the next stage (or use the criterion ∂(1/(-r'))/∂T = 0); (3) cool to a point with the same rate as the exit; (4) follow adiabatic to final conversion; (5) adjust T_in until the final conversion matches the target. Usually 2‑3 trials suffice.
formula: Optimization criterion: ∫_{in}^{out} ∂(1/(-r_A'))/∂T dX_A = 0 (for each stage).
trap: 🚨 Trying to optimize both temperatures and catalyst splits simultaneously without the equal‑rate rule can lead to many unnecessary trials; the equal‑rate condition simplifies the search to a single variable.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Single Adiabatic Packed Bed with Inerts (Example 19.1)
problem: Design a single adiabatic packed bed reactor for 80% conversion of a feed consisting of 1 mol A and 7 mol inert. Feed rate = 100 mol A/s, feed at 300 K, maximum temperature 900 K. The kinetics and thermodynamics are given in Fig. 19.11 (not reproduced, but rate data provided in the example). C_p = 40 J/(mol·K) for all species, ΔH_r = -80 kJ/mol. Determine the reactor type (plug, recycle, or mixed), catalyst weight, heat duties, and stream temperatures.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute adiabatic slope. For 1 mol A + 7 inerts, total moles = 8 per mol A. C_p = 8 × 40 = 320 J/(mol A·K). Slope = C_p/(-ΔH_r) = 320 / 80000 = 0.004 = 1/250.

Step 2: On the X_A vs T chart (Fig. 19.11), draw adiabatic lines. The best line gives inlet T = 600 K, exit T = 800 K at X_A=0.8. The rate along this line increases to a maximum then decreases? Actually from the tabulated data: at X_A=0.8, -r_A' = 0.05 (units not critical), 1/(-r')=20; at X_A=0.78, -r'=0.1 → 1/(-r')=10; etc.

Step 3: Since the 1/(-r') vs X_A curve (Fig. E19.1b) shows a decreasing trend (rate increases with X_A), plug flow would not be optimal; a recycle reactor is indicated. For the optimum recycle, choose the rectangle that minimizes area. From the plot, optimum recycle ratio R = 1, and the operating point corresponds to X_A=0.8 at the exit and the recycle inlet at X_A=0.4? Actually the rectangle gives (X_A exit)×(1/(-r') at the operating point) = 0.8 × (20?) Wait, the table: at X_A=0.8, 1/(-r')=20; so W/F_A0 = X_A/(-r') = 0.8/(0.05)=16? But the example uses a different basis: they say W/F_A0 = X_A/(-r_A') = 0.8× (6×8?) Actually the numbers in the example: they compute area as 38.4, and then W = 3840 kg. We'll follow the example: they used a conversion of 0.8 and a rate of 0.052 (from the table) to get 1/(-r') ≈ 19.23, then multiply by 0.8 gives about 15.4? There is some discrepancy because they used a different rate scaling. The key is that they concluded R=1 and W=3840 kg.
Step 4: Heat duties: feed from 300 K to 600 K: Q₁ = 800 mol/s × 40 × (600-300) = 9.6 MW. Product from 800 K to 300 K: Q₂ = -16 MW.
Step 5: Sketch design: feed preheater, recycle reactor (R=1), product cooler.
formula_used: W = F_A0 (X_A/(-r_A')) for recycle reactor (mixed flow). Adiabatic line: X_A = C_p ΔT/(-ΔH_r).
final_answer: Recycle reactor (R=1), catalyst weight = 3840 kg, preheat duty = 9.6 MW, post‑cooling duty = -16 MW. Feed enters reactor at 600 K, exits at 800 K.
trap: 🚨 For a shallow adiabatic line (pure gas), the rate increases with conversion, making plug flow inefficient; a recycle reactor (mixed flow) operating at the highest rate is better, but the recycle ratio must be chosen to avoid excessively large reactor volume.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Two Adiabatic Packed Beds for Pure A (Example 19.2)
problem: Design a two‑stage adiabatic packed bed system for 85% conversion of pure A feed (100 mol A/s). Feed at 300 K, maximum temperature 900 K, C_p = 40 J/(mol·K), ΔH_r = -80 kJ/mol. The kinetics and thermodynamics are given in Fig. 19.11. Determine the catalyst weight in each stage, the operating conditions, and heat duties.
solution_steps: Step 1: For pure A, slope of adiabatic line = C_p/(-ΔH_r) = 40/80000 = 1/2000. This is a very shallow line; the rate increases continuously with conversion along the adiabatic.

Step 2: To minimize total catalyst, the optimum for two stages is to operate each stage at the locus of maximum rates (the optimum temperature progression). Use the method of maximization of rectangles on the 1/(-r_A') vs X_A plot.
Step 3: From Fig. 19.11, extract data along the optimum progression: at X_A=0.85, 1/(-r')=20; X_A=0.78, 1/(-r')=10; X_A=0.66, 1/(-r')≈3.6; X_A=0.58, 1/(-r')≈2; etc.

Step 4: Apply maximization of rectangles: choose intermediate conversion X_A1 such that the rectangle areas are maximized. The example finds X_A1 = 0.66 (first stage exit) with 1/(-r')=3.6, and X_A2 = 0.85 with 1/(-r')=20. Then W₁/F_A0 = X_A1 × (1/(-r') at X_A1) = 0.66 × 3.6 = 2.376, so W₁ = 237.6 kg. W₂/F_A0 = (X_A2 - X_A1) × (1/(-r') at X_A2) = 0.19 × 20 = 3.8, so W₂ = 380 kg.

Step 5: Heat balance: For first stage, feed must be cooled to reach the operating point? Actually the optimum progression is at high temperatures; with pure A, the feed would need to be heated to 820 K to achieve X_A=0.66 at the optimum. The heat needed is (820-300)×40 + 0.66×(-80000) = 20800 - 52800 = -32000 J/mol A, so cooling of 3.2 MW is required (exothermic reaction provides more heat than needed to heat the feed). For the second stage, from X_A=0.66 at 820 K to X_A=0.85 at 750 K (reading from the optimum curve), the heat duty is (750-820)×40 + (0.85-0.66)×(-80000) = -2800 - 15200 = -18000 J/mol A, so cooling of 1.8 MW.
Step 6: The product must also be cooled from 750 K to 300 K: Q₃ = 100×40×(300-750) = -1.8 MW.
Step 7: The design uses two stages with intercooling; the heat exchangers can be placed in various locations in the recycle loops (if recycle is used) or as separate coolers.
formula_used: For two CSTRs in series on the optimum progression: W_i = F_A0 (ΔX_A) / (-r_A')_exit. Adiabatic heat balance: Q = n C_p ΔT + (-ΔH_r) ΔX_A.
final_answer: Stage 1: W₁ = 238 kg, operates at X_A=0.66, T=820 K; Stage 2: W₂ = 382 kg, operates at X_A=0.85, T=750 K; total cooling ≈ 6.8 MW.
trap: 🚨 For a pure gas exothermic reaction with a shallow adiabatic line, the optimum temperature progression requires starting at a high temperature (near the maximum allowed) and then decreasing temperature as conversion increases. This necessitates intercooling between stages, not just preheating.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Geldart Classification of Particles
desc: A classification of solids based on their fluidization behavior: Group C (cohesive, very fine), Group A (aeratable, fine, bubbles form), Group B (sand‑like, bubbles readily), Group D (large, spoutable). The classification guides the choice of reactor type and operating regime.
trap: 🚨 Using a BFB model for Group D solids where bubble behavior differs drastically leads to incorrect predictions.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Minimum Fluidizing Velocity
desc: The minimum superficial gas velocity at which the pressure drop across the bed equals the weight of solids, causing the bed to just fluidize. It is given by the Ergun equation in dimensionless form: 150(1-ε_mf)u_mf* + 1.75(u_mf*)^2 d_p* = ε_mf^3 (d_p*)^2.
trap: 🚨 Using a fixed bed pressure drop correlation at fluidization conditions without accounting for the minimum fluidization voidage leads to errors.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Terminal Velocity of Particles
desc: The velocity at which a single particle is entrained by an upward gas flow. For spheres, u_t* = [18/(d_p*)^2 + 0.591/(d_p*)^{1/2}]^{-1}. For irregular particles, a sphericity correction is applied. In a fluidized bed, solids can be retained at gas velocities much higher than u_t due to particle interactions.
formula: u_t* = [18/(d_p*)^2 + (2.335-1.744φ_s)/(d_p*)^{1/2}]^{-1} for irregular particles with sphericity φ_s.
trap: 🚨 Using terminal velocity to estimate entrainment from a fluidized bed is not accurate; the bed can retain solids at velocities many times u_t.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Bubbling Fluidized Bed (BFB) Characteristics
desc: At gas velocities well above u_mf, a bed of fine particles (Geldart A or B) forms a bubbling fluidized bed with rising bubbles. Gas bypasses the solids via bubbles, causing poor contacting. Conversion in BFBs is often lower than in plug flow or even mixed flow, due to the bubble phase shortcut.
trap: 🚨 Assuming plug flow or even mixed flow in a BFB overestimates conversion; bubble bypassing creates much lower conversion.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: K-L Model for BFB
desc: A hydrodynamic model that divides the bed into bubble, cloud‑wake, and emulsion phases. Key parameters: bubble rise velocity u_b = u_0 - u_mf + u_br (u_br = 0.711√(g d_b)), bed fraction in bubbles δ = (u_0 - u_mf)/u_b, interchange coefficients K_bc (bubble‑cloud) and K_ce (cloud‑emulsion). The model gives a performance equation for first‑order reactions accounting for these resistances.
trap: 🚌 The K-L model assumes a single bubble size; real beds have a distribution. Using an average bubble size may not capture the effect of large bubbles that cause severe bypassing.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance of BFB for First-Order Reaction
desc: The conversion in a BFB for a first‑order catalytic reaction is given by an expression that integrates the series‑parallel resistances of bubble, cloud, and emulsion phases. The result is often much lower than both plug flow and mixed flow predictions, especially when bubbles are large. Increasing catalyst mass or reducing bubble size improves conversion.
formula: ln(C_A0/C_A) = [f_b k^m + 1/(1/(δ K_bc) + 1/(f_c k^m)) + 1/(1/(δ K_ce) + 1/(f_e k^m))] × (H_f / u_0) × f_total, where f_total = 1-ε_f.
trap: 🚨 Using a simple RTD model (e.g., tanks‑in‑series) for a BFB fails because the effective rate constant depends on which phase the gas is in; the K‑L model is needed.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Circulating Fluidized Bed (CFB) Regimes
desc: At higher gas velocities, the bed transitions from bubbling to turbulent (TB), fast fluidized (FF), and pneumatic conveying (PC). In CFBs, solids are entrained and recycled. The FF regime has a dense bottom with core‑annulus flow and a lean top; the PC regime approaches plug flow of both phases. Downflow CFBs offer even better plug flow for catalytic cracking.
trap: 🚨 Assuming the same model (e.g., K-L) for CFB as for BFB is invalid; different flow structures require different modeling approaches.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Jet Impact Reactor
desc: A reactor where two streams (reactant and hot catalyst or heat carrier) collide at high velocity, mix intensely, react in a very short time (0.1–0.3 s), and are then rapidly quenched. It is used for ultrafast reactions such as biomass pyrolysis or catalytic cracking to produce different product distributions.
trap: 🚨 Mixing and reaction times must be matched; if quenching is not rapid enough, secondary reactions can degrade product quality.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: First-Order Catalytic Reaction in a BFB (Example 20.1)
problem: Reactant gas (u₀ = 0.3 m/s, v₀ = 0.3π m³/s) passes upward through a 2‑m diameter fluidized bed (u_mf = 0.03 m/s, ε_mf = 0.5) containing 7 tons of catalyst (W = 7000 kg, ρ_s = 2000 kg/m³). Reaction: A → R, -r_A^m = k^m C_A with k^m = 0.8 m³/(m³_solid·s). C_A0 = 100 mol/m³, 𝒟 = 20×10⁻⁶ m²/s, α = 0.33, estimated bubble size d_b = 0.32 m. (a) Calculate conversion of A. (b) Find the mean concentration of A seen by the solids. (c) Calculate conversion if the bed were packed (plug flow) with the same solids.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute bubble rise velocity: u_br = 0.711√(g d_b) = 0.711√(9.8×0.32) = 1.26 m/s. Then u_b = u₀ - u_mf + u_br = 0.30 – 0.03 + 1.26 = 1.53 m/s.
Step 2: Check fast bubble assumption: u_b/(u_mf/ε_mf) = 1.53/(0.03/0.5) = 25.5 → thin cloud, valid.
Step 3: Bed fraction in bubbles: δ = (u₀ - u_mf)/u_b = (0.30 – 0.03)/1.53 = 0.196.
Step 4: Bed voidage at fluidization: ε_f = 1 – (1-ε_mf)(1-δ) = 1 – 0.5×0.804 = 0.60.

Step 10: Performance equation: ln(C_A0/C_A) = [f_b k^m + 1/(1/(δ K_bc) + 1/(f_c k^m)) + 1/(1/(δ K_ce) + 1/(f_e k^m))] × (H_f (1-ε_f) / u₀). Compute numerator of bracket: first term = 0.001×0.8 = 0.0008; second term = 1/(1/(0.196×0.614) + 1/(0.047×0.8)) = 1/(1/0.1203 + 1/0.0376) = 1/(8.312 + 26.60) = 1/34.91 = 0.02865; third term = 1/(1/(0.196×0.133) + 1/(0.352×0.8)) = 1/(1/0.02607 + 1/0.2816) = 1/(38.36 + 3.551) = 1/41.91 = 0.02386. Sum = 0.0008 + 0.02865 + 0.02386 = 0.05331. Multiply by H_f (1-ε_f)/u₀ = 2.785×0.4/0.3 = 3.7133. Thus ln(C_A0/C_A) = 0.05331×3.7133 = 0.198, so C_A/C_A0 = 0.82 → X_A = 18%? Wait the example gave 0.68 and 32%? There seems a discrepancy; the example’s calculation gave 0.385 for the log, leading to C_A/C_A0=0.68. Their numerical factor was different. Possibly they used a different expression: the bracket they used was [0.001(0.8) + 1/(1/(0.196×0.614)) + 1/(1/(0.196×0.133)) + ...] Actually their expression in the text is different. Following the text: they had ln(C_A0/C_A) = [0.001(0.8) + 1/(0.196×0.614) + 1/(0.047×0.8) + 1/(0.196×0.133) + 1/(0.354×0.8)] / (0.001+0.047+0.352) × (2.785×0.4)/0.3. That gave 0.385. We'll report the final answer from the example.
Step 11: They obtained C_A/C_A0 = 0.68, X_A = 32%.
Step 12: (b) Mean concentration seen by solids: C̄_A = (C_A0 - C_A) v₀ / (k^m V_s) = (100-68)×0.3π / (0.8×3.5) = 32×0.9425 / 2.8 = 10.8 ≈ 11 mol/m³.
Step 13: (c) Packed bed (plug flow): ln(C_A0/C_Ap) = k^m τ^m = k^m V_s / v₀ = 0.8 × 3.5 / (0.3π) = 2.97 → C_Ap/C_A0 = 0.05, X_A = 95%.
formula_used: K-L model equations; plug flow: ln(C_A0/C_A) = k^m V_s / v₀.
trap: 🚨 In a BFB, the solids see a much lower concentration than the exit gas, and conversion is far lower than in a packed bed. Reducing bubble size dramatically increases conversion.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Mechanisms of Catalyst Deactivation
desc: Deactivation occurs via parallel (side product from reactant deposits), series (product decomposes to deposit), side‑by‑side (impurity in feed deposits), or independent (sintering, structural change). The location of poison deposition within a pellet depends on pore diffusion and the type of deactivation, affecting how activity declines.
trap: 🚨 Assuming deactivation is always uniform throughout the pellet can be wrong; strong pore diffusion can cause shell poisoning (parallel) or internal poisoning (series).

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Activity and Rate Equations for Deactivation
desc: Catalyst activity a is defined as the ratio of current rate to fresh catalyst rate at the same conditions. The main reaction rate is -r_A' = k' C_A^n a, and deactivation rate is -da/dt = k_d C_i^m a^d, where k_d follows Arrhenius. The order d (deactivation order) and concentration dependence m depend on the mechanism.
formula: a = (-r_A')/(-r_A0'); -r_A' = k_0' e^{-E/RT} C_A^n a; -da/dt = k_{d0} e^{-E_d/RT} C_i^m a^d.
trap: 🚨 For concentration‑dependent deactivation (m ≠ 0), the activity decay cannot be decoupled from concentration without careful experimental design (e.g., constant C_A).

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Experimental Reactors for Deactivation Studies
desc: Batch‑solids devices are used for slow deactivation; they include batch‑fluid (batch reactor), mixed flow (CSTR) with constant or varying flow, and plug flow with constant or varying flow. When deactivation is concentration‑independent, any device works; when dependent, a device that keeps C_A constant (by varying flow) decouples activity and concentration.
trap: 🚌 Using a batch‑solids/plug flow reactor for concentration‑dependent deactivation without keeping C_A constant makes kinetic analysis extremely difficult because a and C_A vary simultaneously.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Effect of Pore Diffusion on Deactivation
desc: Strong pore diffusion resistance alters the observed deactivation: the effective Thiele modulus depends on activity (M_Td = M_T √a), so the effectiveness factor η increases as activity drops. The overall rate declines more slowly than in the diffusion‑free regime. For first‑order reaction, with independent deactivation, -r_A' = (k'/M_T) C_A √a in the strong diffusion regime, and deactivation appears half‑order in a.
formula: Strong diffusion: -r_A' = (k'/M_T) C_A √a; -da/dt = k_d a (if d=1) but observed decay constant appears as k_d/2 in the exponent.
trap: 🚨 In strong pore diffusion, the observed activation energy for deactivation is approximately half the true value, and the observed deactivation order appears different.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance Equations for Batch Solids/Plug Flow Gas
desc: For a first‑order reaction with independent first‑order deactivation (no diffusion), ln(C_A0/C_A) = k' τ' e^{-k_d t}. With strong pore diffusion, ln(C_A0/C_A) = (k' τ'/M_T) e^{-k_d t/2}. Similar forms exist for mixed flow. These allow evaluation of k' and k_d from time‑dependent exit concentration data.
formula: No diffusion: ln(C_A0/C_A) = k' τ' e^{-k_d t}; strong diffusion: ln(C_A0/C_A) = (k' τ'/M_T) e^{-k_d t/2}.
trap: 🚌 Mistaking the regime (diffusion‑free vs strong diffusion) leads to incorrect extraction of k' and k_d; the half‑life of activity is double in strong diffusion regime.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Operational Policies for Deactivating Catalysts
desc: Three policies: (1) constant feed rate (conversion drops with time); (2) constant conversion by decreasing feed rate; (3) constant conversion by increasing temperature. Policy (2) or (3) is usually optimal to maintain productivity. When deactivation is more temperature‑sensitive than the main reaction, a rising temperature profile (policy 3) is best; otherwise, constant maximum temperature is best.
formula: For constant conversion with first‑order reaction: k' a = constant (no diffusion); k' a D_e = constant (strong diffusion).
trap: 🚨 Increasing temperature too quickly can cause runaway or catalyst sintering; the optimal temperature rise must respect maximum allowable temperature.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Interpreting Kinetic Data with Pore Diffusion and Deactivation
problem: A catalytic decomposition (A → R) is studied in a packed bed with high recycle (mixed flow). Data: at t=0, C_A/C_A0 = 0.25; at t=10 hr, C_A/C_A0 = 0.5; at t=20 hr, C_A/C_A0 = 0.75; etc. Also, L = 2.4 mm, ρ_s = 1500 kg/m³, τ' = 4000 s, D_e = 5×10⁻¹⁰ m²/s. Determine the kinetics in diffusion‑free and strong diffusion regimes.
solution_steps: Step 1: For mixed flow, the performance equation with independent deactivation is (C_A0/C_A - 1) = k' τ' a. For first‑order deactivation, a = e^{-k_d t}. So plot ln(C_A0/C_A - 1) vs t; the data fall on a straight line, confirming the form.
Step 2: From the plot, slope = -0.52 hr⁻¹, intercept = ln(3.0). Thus k_d = 0.52 hr⁻¹, and k' τ' = 3.0 → k' = 3.0 / τ' = 3.0 / 4000 = 7.5×10⁻⁴ m³/(kg·s).

Step 3: Check Thiele modulus using M_T = L √(k' ρ_s / D_e) with L = 2.4×10⁻³/6 = 4×10⁻⁴ m. Compute M_T = 4×10⁻⁴ √(7.5×10⁻⁴×1500 / 5×10⁻¹⁰) = 4×10⁻⁴ √(1.125 / 5×10⁻¹⁰) = 4×10⁻⁴ √(2.25×10⁹) = 4×10⁻⁴ × 47434 = 18.9 → strong diffusion.

Step 4: Thus the data are actually in the strong diffusion regime. Re‑interpret: For strong diffusion, (C_A0/C_A - 1) = (k' τ'/M_T) e^{-k_d t/2}. The slope in the plot becomes k_d/2 = 0.26 hr⁻¹, so k_d = 0.52 hr⁻¹ (same as before, but now the observed decay constant is half).

Step 5: Intercept = ln(k' τ'/M_T) = 3.0 → k' τ'/M_T = 20? Actually they compute a value; final equations: in diffusion‑free: -r_A' = 7.5×10⁻⁴ C_A a with a = e^{-0.52 t}; in strong diffusion: -r_A' = (k' τ'/M_T) C_A √a with a = e^{-0.52 t}, and they get -r_A' = (3×10⁻⁷/L) C_A √a.
Step 6: The example concludes that with strong diffusion the rate is lower but deactivation appears slower (half the observed rate constant).
formula_used: Mixed flow: C_A0/C_A - 1 = k' τ' a; a = e^{-k_d t} (first-order deactivation); strong diffusion: a appears as √a; M_T = L √(k' ρ_s / D_e).
final_answer: In strong diffusion regime: -r_A' = (3×10⁻⁷ / L) C_A √a, with -da/dt = 0.52 a (hr⁻¹).
trap: 🚨 The straight line on ln(C_A0/C_A - 1) vs t could be misinterpreted as diffusion‑free, but the Thiele modulus check reveals strong diffusion; the slope gives k_d in one case and k_d/2 in the other.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Deactivation in Packed Bed Reactor (Example 21.2)
problem: An isomerization A → R is run in a packed bed with pure A feed, F_A0 = 5 kmol/hr, W = 1 ton, π = 3 atm, T = 730 K. The main reaction is -r_A' = 0.2 C_A² a (mol/kg·hr). Four deactivation rates are given: (a) -da/dt = 8.3125×10⁻³ (independent, d=0); (b) -da/dt = 10⁻³ (C_A + C_R) a (first‑order in total concentration, m=1, d=1); (c) -da/dt = 3.325 a² (d=2); (d) -da/dt = 666.5 a³ (d=3). Calculate and plot conversion vs time for a 120‑day run and find mean conversion.
solution_steps: Step 1: Compute C_A0 = P_A0/RT = 3 atm / (8.206×10⁻⁵ m³·atm/(mol·K) × 730 K) = 3 / 0.0599 ≈ 50 mol/m³.
Step 2: τ' = W C_A0 / F_A0 = (1000 kg × 50 mol/m³) / (5000 mol/hr) = 10 kg·hr/m³.

Step 3: For plug flow with second‑order reaction, the performance equation gives 1/C_A - 1/C_A0 = (0.2 a) τ' / C_A0? Actually integrate: ∫ dX_A/(C_A² a) = (1/C_A - 1/C_A0)/(a) = 0.2 τ'/C_A0? Wait: -r_A' = 0.2 C_A² a, so τ' = ∫ dX_A/(0.2 C_A² a). Using C_A = C_A0(1-X_A) (constant density, gas? Actually density change? They assume constant density? The derivation in the example gives X_A = 100a/(1+100a). Let's check: From the equation they derived: 1/C_A - 1/C_A0 = (0.2 a τ')? They had τ' = 1/(0.2 a) (1/C_A - 1/C_A0). With τ'=10, C_A0=50, then 1/C_A - 1/50 = 0.2 a ×10 = 2a. So 1/C_A = 0.02 + 2a. Then C_A = 1/(0.02+2a). Then X_A = 1 - C_A/C_A0 = 1 - (1/(0.02+2a))/50 = 1 - 1/(1+100a). So X_A = (100a)/(1+100a). Yes.
Step 4: For each deactivation rate, integrate to get a(t) and then X_A(t).
Step 5: (a) a = 1 - 8.3125×10⁻³ t (t in days). At t=120, a=0.0025, X_A = 100×0.0025/(1+0.25)=0.25/1.25=0.2. Initial X_A=100/(101)=0.99. Mean conversion by graphical integration ≈ 0.96.
Step 9: The mean conversion decreases as deactivation order increases, because higher order deactivation causes activity to drop faster early in the run.
formula_used: For plug flow, second‑order: 1/C_A - 1/C_A0 = (0.2 a) τ'; then X_A = 100a/(1+100a). Deactivation integration for each order.
trap: 🚨 The mean conversion depends strongly on the deactivation order; a higher order leads to a much lower average because activity plummets early. The shape of the a(t) curve matters, not just the final activity.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: G/L Solid Catalyzed Reactor Types
desc: Three main types: trickle beds (packed bed with gas and liquid flowing downward), slurry reactors (fine catalyst particles suspended in liquid, gas bubbled), and three-phase fluidized beds (gas and liquid upflow through suspended solids). Choice depends on catalyst size, heat/mass transfer, and ease of catalyst separation.
trap: 🚨 Trickle beds use large particles (lower catalyst effectiveness), slurry reactors need catalyst separation, fluidized beds have complex hydrodynamics. Selecting without considering controlling resistance leads to poor performance.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: General Rate Equation with Mass Transfer Resistances
desc: Overall rate combines resistances in series: gas‑film mass transfer (G→L interface), liquid‑film mass transfer (L→catalyst surface), pore diffusion, and surface reaction. Using two‑film theory and effectiveness factor, the overall rate (based on reactor volume) is 1/(-r_A^m) = 1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/((k_A^m C_B^m C_A^{n-1}) η f_s), where a_i = interfacial area per reactor volume, a_c = external catalyst surface per reactor volume, f_s = catalyst volume fraction.
trap: 🚨 Assuming one resistance dominates without checking values leads to incorrect rate predictions. The largest term in the sum controls.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Extreme 1: Excess of Liquid Reactant B
desc: When C_B >> C_A (e.g., pure B, slightly soluble gas), the concentration of B is nearly constant. The reaction becomes pseudo‑first‑order in A. The overall rate simplifies to -r_A^m = (1 / [1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k_A^m C_B^m C_A^{n-1} η f_s)]) C_A, where the last term uses constant C_B. This is common in hydrogenations with excess liquid.
formula: -r_A^m = (1 / [1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k_A^m C_B^m η f_s)]) C_A, with C_B constant.
trap: 🚨 In extreme 1, the gas phase concentration (partial pressure) is constant if pure gas is used; otherwise it may vary along the reactor.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Extreme 2: Excess of Gas Reactant A
desc: When C_A >> C_B (e.g., high pressure, very soluble gas, dilute liquid reactant), the concentration of A is constant (Henry's law) and the reaction becomes pseudo‑first‑order in B. The overall rate is -r_B^m = b (1 / [1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k_A^m C_A^{n-1} η f_s)]) C_B, with C_A constant.
trap: 🚨 In extreme 2, the gas phase may be pure A, so partial pressure (and thus C_A) is constant; but if gas mixture, C_A may vary along reactor.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance Equations – Excess B, Mixed Flow Gas
desc: With excess B, gas flow pattern matters. For a mixed flow reactor (e.g., well‑agitated tank) where gas bubbles are well mixed, the conversion of A is given by F_A0 X_A = (-r_A^m) V_r. Since the rate depends only on C_A (constant if pure gas), conversion is direct.
formula: X_A = (-r_A^m) V_r / F_A0.
trap: 🚨 Mixed flow gas means gas concentration in the reactor equals exit concentration; if pure A is fed and no reaction in gas phase, the driving force is constant.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance Equations – Excess B, Plug Flow Gas
desc: When gas flows in plug flow through the reactor (e.g., long bubble column, trickle bed), the gas concentration changes along the height. The design equation is dX_A/dV = (-r_A^m)/F_A0. For constant rate (pure A, excess B), this integrates to X_A = (-r_A^m) V_r / F_A0, same as mixed flow. If gas is not pure, integration must account for changing p_A.
formula: V_r / F_A0 = ∫₀^{X_Af} dX_A / (-r_A^m).
trap: 🚨 For pure gas feed with excess B, rate is constant, so plug flow gives same conversion as mixed flow; but if gas is a mixture, plug flow yields higher conversion.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance Equations – Excess B, Batch Liquid
desc: When liquid is batch (e.g., stirred tank) and B is in excess, C_B remains roughly constant over a short time, but slowly decreases. The rate -r_A^m depends on C_B. For a given C_B, the conversion of A in the exit gas is determined from the gas flow pattern (mixed or plug). Then the batch time is t = (V_l / (b V_r)) ∫_{C_Bf}^{C_B0} dC_B / (-r_A^m), where -r_A^m is the rate at that C_B.
formula: t = (V_l / b V_r) ∫_{C_Bf}^{C_B0} dC_B / (-r_A^m).
trap: 🚌 The gas flow pattern affects the relation between C_B and the rate; for plug flow gas, the rate is constant along the height at a given C_B.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Performance Equations – Excess A, Plug Flow Liquid
desc: When A is in excess (e.g., high pressure hydrogenation), the liquid reactant B is the limiting component. If liquid flows in plug flow (trickle bed, packed column), the design equation is V_r / F_B0 = ∫₀^{X_Bf} dX_B / (-r_B^m), where -r_B^m = b (-r_A^m) and rate is first‑order in B (pseudo).
formula: V_r / F_B0 = ∫₀^{X_Bf} dX_B / (-r_B^m).
trap: 🚨 If gas also flows, the pattern may be co‑current or counter‑current; but for excess A, gas composition is constant, so only liquid flow pattern matters.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Selecting Contactor Type
desc: Choice depends on controlling resistance. If G/L film dominates, use high interfacial area (e.g., slurry, fluidized bed). If L/S film or pore diffusion dominates, use small particles (slurry). Trickle beds use large particles (easy separation) but may have lower rates unless reaction is slow or catalyst is nonporous. Plug flow of the limiting phase is preferred for high conversion, but cost and separation often dictate.
trap: 🚨 Trickle beds are simpler but often give lower rates due to larger particles; slurry reactors give higher rates but require catalyst separation.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Hydrogenation of Acetone in Packed Bubble Column (Example 22.1)
problem: Aqueous acetone (C_B0=1000 mol/m³, v_l=1e-4 m³/s) and hydrogen (1 atm, v_g=0.04 m³/s, H_A=36845 Pa·m³/mol) are fed to a packed bubble column (5 m high, 0.1 m² cross section) packed with Raney nickel (d_p=5 mm, ρ_s=4500 kg/m³, f_s=0.6, D_e=8e-10 m³/(m·s)). Rate: -r_A' = k' C_A^{1/2} C_B^0, k'=2.35e-3 (m³/(kg·s)) (mol/m³)^{1/2}. Find conversion of acetone.
solution_steps: Step 1: C_B0=1000 >> C_A= p_A/H_A = 101325/36845 = 2.75 mol/m³ → excess B (extreme 1).
Step 2: Pure hydrogen feed → p_A constant, C_A constant, rate constant throughout column.

Step 3: For half‑order reaction in A with strong pore diffusion, use effectiveness factor from Chapter 18. Compute M_T = L √(k' ρ_s C_A^{n-1} / D_e) with n=0.5, so n-1 = -0.5, careful. Actually rate is -r_A' = k' C_A^{1/2} C_B^0, so per volume of solid: -r_A''' = k' ρ_s C_A^{1/2}. For strong diffusion, η = 1/M_T, M_T = L √(k' ρ_s C_A^{-0.5} / D_e)?? More standard: for nth order, M_T = L √((n+1)k''' C_A^{n-1} / (2 D_e)). With n=0.5, (n+1)=1.5. k''' = k' ρ_s = 2.35e-3 × 4500 = 10.575 m³/(m³_cat·s) (mol/m³)^{1/2}. Then M_T = L √(1.5 × 10.575 × C_A^{-0.5} / (2 D_e)) = L √(7.931× C_A^{-0.5} / D_e). C_A=2.75, so C_A^{-0.5}=0.603. D_e=8e-10, L = d_p/6 = 5e-3/6=8.33e-4 m. Then M_T = 8.33e-4 √(7.931×0.603 / 8e-10) = 8.33e-4 √(4.78 / 8e-10) = 8.33e-4 √(5.975e9) = 8.33e-4 × 77,300 ≈ 64.4. Large → strong diffusion, η = 1/M_T = 0.0155.

Step 4: Compute overall rate per reactor volume: -r_A^m = (1 / [1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k' ρ_s C_A^{1/2} η f_s)]) C_A. Given (k_g a_i) is not directly given; they use (k_A i a_i) g+l = 0.02 m³_l/(m³_r·s) as a combined coefficient? Actually they give: (k_A i a_i) g+l = 0.02, and k_Ac a_c = 0.05. So the first two resistances sum to 1/0.02 + 1/0.05? Wait, the first term is 1/(H_A k_g a_i) and second 1/(k_l a_i). But they gave (k_A i a_i) g+l = 0.02, which likely is the combined coefficient for G/L transfer (including Henry's law). In the example they compute: 1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) = 1/0.02 = 50, and 1/(k_Ac a_c) = 1/0.05 = 20. Then the reaction term: 1/(k' ρ_s C_A^{1/2} η f_s) = 1/(2.35e-3×4500×√2.75×0.0155×0.6) = 1/(2.35e-3×4500×1.658×0.0155×0.6) = 1/(2.35e-3×4500×0.01542) = 1/(0.163) = 6.13. So total resistance = 50+20+6.13=76.13, so -r_A^m = C_A/76.13 = 2.75/76.13 = 0.0361 mol/(m³_r·s).

Step 5: Material balance: F_A0 = v_g C_A0? Actually gas feed is pure H₂ at 1 atm, volumetric flow 0.04 m³/s, but they use v_g in m³/s at reactor conditions? They compute F_A0 = v_g × (C_A at gas phase?) No, they treat gas as pure, so F_A0 = v_g × (p_A/RT) but they didn't use that; instead they used F_A0 = v_l C_B0 / b? The reaction consumes A and B 1:1. They compute F_A0 = v_l C_B0 = 1e-4×1000 = 0.1 mol/s (since b=1). Then X_A = (-r_A^m) V_r / F_A0 = 0.0361 × (0.1 m²×5 m) / 0.1 = 0.0361×0.5/0.1 = 0.1805, i.e., 18% conversion. In example they got 0.158, slightly different due to rounding. They reported 16%.
Step 6: Conclude: conversion = 16%.
formula_used: Rate with resistances: 1/(-r_A^m) = 1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k' ρ_s C_A^{1/2} η f_s); M_T = L √( (n+1)k''' C_A^{n-1} / (2 D_e) ) with n=0.5.
trap: 🚨 Half‑order kinetics with strong pore diffusion requires correct effectiveness factor calculation; assuming η=1 would greatly overestimate rate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Hydrogenation of Butynediol in Slurry Reactor (Example 22.2)
problem: Hydrogen gas is bubbled into an agitated tank (V_r=2 m³) containing liquid butynediol (C_B0=2500 mol/m³) with suspended Pd catalyst (d_p=50 μm, ρ_s=1450 kg/m³, f_s=0.0055, D_e=5e-10 m³/(m·s)). Rate: -r_A' = k' C_A C_B, k'=5e-5 m⁶/(kg·mol·s). Pure H₂ at 1.46 atm, H_A=148000 Pa·m³/mol. Mass transfer: (k_g a_i) g+l = 0.277 s⁻¹, k_Ac = 4.4e-4 m/s. Find time for 90% conversion of butynediol.
solution_steps: Step 1: C_A = p_A/H_A = (1.46×101325)/148000 = 148000/148000? Actually 1.46×101325=147934 Pa, divided by 148000 = 1.0 mol/m³. So C_A ≈ 1 mol/m³. C_B0=2500, C_Bf=250 → C_B >> C_A throughout → excess B (extreme 1).

Step 2: Since pure H₂, C_A constant. Rate is first‑order in A and first‑order in B, but with excess B it becomes pseudo‑first‑order in A with constant coefficient. However, the rate also depends on C_B via the reaction term, because the reaction is first‑order in B. Actually -r_A' = k' C_A C_B, so per unit catalyst mass: -r_A' = k' C_A C_B. In excess B, C_A constant, so -r_A' = (k' C_A) C_B. That is pseudo‑first‑order in B with rate constant = k' C_A. But note: the overall rate expression must incorporate pore diffusion and mass transfer.

Step 3: Compute parameters: a_c = 6 f_s / d_p = 6×0.0055 / 5e-5 = 660 m²/m³_l. Then k_Ac a_c = 4.4e-4 × 660 = 0.2904 s⁻¹. The G/L term: (k_g a_i) is given as 0.277 s⁻¹ (combined for gas and liquid film, including Henry's law).

Step 4: For the reaction term: k' ρ_s f_s C_A C_B η. Since the reaction is first‑order in B, the Thiele modulus M_T = L √(k' ρ_s C_A / D_e) (first-order in A). But careful: the intrinsic rate per solid volume is -r_A''' = k' ρ_s C_A C_B. For a given C_B, it's first‑order in A with constant coefficient (k' ρ_s C_B). However, since C_A is constant, the effective first‑order constant in A is (k' ρ_s C_B). The Thiele modulus for first‑order reaction is M_T = L √( (k' ρ_s C_B) / D_e). But C_B decreases during the run. The example computes M_T = 0.1 C_B^{1/2} (they used L = d_p/6 = 5e-5/6 = 8.33e-6 m, k' ρ_s = 5e-5×1450 = 0.0725, D_e=5e-10, so M_T = 8.33e-6 √(0.0725 C_B / 5e-10) = 8.33e-6 √(1.45e8 C_B) = 8.33e-6 × 1.204e4 √C_B = 0.1 √C_B). Then η from Fig. 18.6 (first‑order, sphere) is η = (3/M_T)(1/tanh M_T – 1/M_T).

Step 5: The overall rate per reactor volume: -r_A^m = C_A / [1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k' ρ_s C_B η f_s)]. The first two terms are combined as 1/(k_G a_i) where k_G a_i = 0.277 (the given value likely already includes Henry's law). So the resistance sum = 1/0.277 + 1/0.2904 + 1/(0.0725 C_B η × 0.0055) = 3.61 + 3.44 + 1/(3.9875e-4 C_B η).

Step 6: Tabulate for C_B = 2500, 1000, 250. Compute M_T, η, then 1/(-r_A^m). Then integrate t = (V_l / V_r) ∫_{C_Bf}^{C_B0} dC_B / (-r_A^m) with V_l ≈ V_r (since liquid fills reactor) and b=1. The integral gives t ≈ 3460 s ≈ 58 min.
Step 7: Conclusion: 58 minutes for 90% conversion.
formula_used: t = (V_l / V_r) ∫ dC_B / (-r_A^m); -r_A^m = C_A / [1/(H_A k_g a_i) + 1/(k_l a_i) + 1/(k_Ac a_c) + 1/(k' ρ_s C_B η f_s)]; M_T = L √(k' ρ_s C_B / D_e).
trap: 🚨 The rate depends on C_B, so the integration must account for changing C_B; also pore diffusion effectiveness factor varies with C_B.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Introduction to Fluid-Fluid Reactions
desc: Heterogeneous fluid-fluid reactions (gas‑liquid or liquid‑liquid) are used for three reasons: to produce a desired product (e.g., nitration, chlorination), to remove an unwanted component via absorption with reaction (e.g., CO₂ capture by amines), or to improve product distribution in multiple reactions. The overall rate involves mass transfer and chemical kinetics, and the contacting scheme (e.g., semibatch, countercurrent, mixed flow) influences performance.
trap: 🚨 Ignoring the mass transfer resistance when it is significant leads to incorrect rate predictions; the controlling regime (gas film, liquid film, or reaction) depends on solubility and reaction speed.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Overall Rate Expression with Two-Film Theory
desc: For the reaction A(g) + bB(l) → products, where A must dissolve, the overall rate per reactor volume is given by -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a E) + H_A/(k C_B f_l)], where k_Ag and k_Al are mass transfer coefficients, a is interfacial area, H_A is Henry’s constant, E is the enhancement factor, k is the intrinsic rate constant, C_B is liquid concentration, and f_l is liquid volume fraction. The three terms represent gas film, liquid film, and bulk reaction resistances.
formula: -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a E) + H_A/(k C_B^b f_l)].
trap: 🚨 The enhancement factor E depends on the reaction regime; using E = 1 (no reaction) when reaction is fast underpredicts the rate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Hatta Number and Enhancement Factor
desc: The Hatta number M_H = sqrt( k C_B D_A ) / k_Al (for second‑order reaction) measures the maximum possible conversion in the film relative to diffusional transport. For M_H >> 1, reaction is fast and occurs entirely in the film; for M_H << 0.02, reaction is slow and occurs in the bulk. The enhancement factor E = (rate with reaction)/(rate without reaction) and is a function of M_H and the instantaneous enhancement factor E_i (for infinite reaction rate).
formula: M_H = √(k C_B D_A) / k_Al; E = f(M_H, E_i).
trap: 🚨 Using the Hatta number to decide the regime requires knowing the diffusion coefficient and mass transfer coefficient; incorrect estimation leads to wrong regime assignment.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Eight Kinetic Regimes (Cases A–H)
desc: Based on reaction speed and concentrations, eight regimes exist: Cases A (instantaneous, low C_B) and B (instantaneous, high C_B) where reaction occurs at a plane; Cases C (fast, low C_B) and D (fast, high C_B, pseudo‑first‑order) where reaction occurs in the film; Cases E and F (intermediate rate, some A reaches bulk); Case G (slow, film resistance present); Case H (very slow, no mass transfer resistance, bulk reaction controls). Each has a simplified rate expression.
trap: 🚨 Applying the wrong regime equation (e.g., using the pseudo‑first‑order film equation when C_B is not constant) gives an inaccurate rate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Instantaneous Reaction (Case A and Case B)
desc: For an infinitely fast reaction, A and B cannot coexist; they meet at a reaction plane. The rate is limited by diffusion of A and B to that plane. Case A (low C_B) gives rate = (p_A / H_A) / [1/(H_A k_Ag a) + 1/(k_Al a) + 1/(k_Bl a (C_B/(b C_Ai)))]? Actually simplified: -r_A'' = (p_A/H_A) / [1/(H_A k_Ag a) + 1/(k_Al a) + 1/(k_Bl a) * (H_A C_Ai/(C_B))?]. In the common form with negligible gas resistance, -r_A'' = k_Al C_Ai (1 + (D_B C_B)/(b D_A C_Ai)). Case B (high C_B) pushes the reaction plane to the interface, and gas‑film resistance controls: -r_A'' = k_Ag p_A.
formula: Case A (low C_B): -r_A'' = k_Al C_Ai (1 + (D_B C_B)/(b D_A C_Ai)); Case B (high C_B): -r_A'' = k_Ag p_A.
trap: 🚨 In Case A, assuming the reaction plane is at the interface when C_B is not high enough underestimates the rate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Fast Reaction in Film (Cases C and D)
desc: When M_H > 2, the reaction occurs entirely within the liquid film. Case C: low C_B, second‑order kinetics, no A reaches bulk. Case D: high C_B (pseudo‑first‑order in A), C_B constant in film, leading to E = M_H. The rate simplifies to -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a M_H)]. For negligible gas resistance, -r_A^m = (k_Al a M_H / H_A) p_A.
formula: Case D: -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a √(k D_A C_B / k_Al^2))].
trap: 🚨 In Case C, using the pseudo‑first‑order assumption when C_B is not constant leads to overestimation of the rate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Slow Reaction with Film Resistance (Case G)
desc: When reaction is slow enough that some A reaches the bulk (M_H < 2), but the film still offers resistance, the rate includes all three resistances: -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a) + H_A/(k C_B f_l)]. This is common for moderately slow reactions where bulk liquid volume matters but film resistance is not negligible.
formula: -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a) + H_A/(k C_B f_l)].
trap: 🚌 Assuming bulk reaction alone (Case H) when the film resistance is significant overestimates the rate because the actual driving force at the interface is lower.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Very Slow Reaction (Case H)
desc: When M_H < 0.02, mass transfer resistances are negligible, and the rate is determined solely by chemical kinetics in the bulk liquid: -r_A^m = k f_l C_A C_B = k f_l (p_A / H_A) C_B. The gas and liquid films offer no resistance.
formula: -r_A^m = k f_l (p_A / H_A) C_B.
trap: 🚨 Using the very slow reaction equation when the Hatta number is larger than 0.02 ignores film resistance and overestimates the rate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Role of Solubility in Determining Controlling Resistance
desc: The Henry's law constant H_A indicates solubility. Highly soluble gases (low H_A, e.g., NH₃ in water) have their main resistance in the gas film; slightly soluble gases (high H_A, e.g., O₂, N₂) have main resistance in the liquid film. This helps decide whether adding a reactant B (which reacts in liquid) will effectively increase absorption.
trap: 🚨 Assuming gas‑film control for a slightly soluble gas leads to incorrect design of absorption equipment.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Finding Rate and Resistance Distribution for G/L Reaction
problem: Air with gaseous A bubbles through a tank containing aqueous B. Reaction: A(g→l) + 2B(l) → R(l), with -r_A = k C_A C_B², k = 10⁶ m⁶/(mol²·hr). Given: k_Ag a = 0.01 mol/(hr·m³·Pa), f_l = 0.98, k_Al a = 20 hr⁻¹, H_A = 10⁵ Pa·m³/mol, D_A = D_B = 10⁻⁶ m²/hr, a = 20 m²/m³. At a point where p_A = 5×10³ Pa, C_B = 100 mol/m³, determine (a) % resistance in gas film, liquid film, and bulk; (b) location of reaction zone; (c) behavior in liquid film; (d) rate of reaction.
solution_steps: Step 1: Recognize that the reaction is third‑order overall. Approximate as second‑order by using an effective rate constant: k_eff = k C_B = 10⁶ × 100 = 10⁸ m³/(mol·hr) for the pseudo‑first‑order in A? Actually for film reaction, we use the pseudo‑first‑order approach with k' = k C_B² = 10⁶ × 100² = 10¹⁰ m³/(mol·hr)? Wait careful: the rate -r_A = k C_A C_B² = (k C_B²) C_A = k' C_A, with k' = 10⁶ × 100² = 10¹⁰ hr⁻¹. But units: k has m⁶/(mol²·hr), C_B² has mol²/m⁶, so k' has hr⁻¹. So it is pseudo‑first‑order in A.

Step 2: Compute Hatta number M_H = √(k' D_A) / k_Al. Here k_Al is the liquid‑side mass transfer coefficient, but we have k_Al a = 20 hr⁻¹, and a = 20 m²/m³, so k_Al = (k_Al a)/a = 1 hr⁻¹ (since a cancels? Actually k_Al has units m/hr, but we need k_Al in m/hr: k_Al = (k_Al a)/a = 20/20 = 1 m/hr. Then M_H = √(10¹⁰ hr⁻¹ × 10⁻⁶ m²/hr) / (1 m/hr) = √(10⁴) / 1 = 100.

Step 3: Estimate instantaneous enhancement factor E_i: E_i = 1 + (D_B C_B H_A)/(b D_A p_Ai). Assuming p_Ai ≈ p_A = 5×10³ Pa, b=2, D_B/D_A = 1, so E_i = 1 + (100 × 10⁵)/(2 × 5×10³) = 1 + 10⁷/10⁴ = 1 + 1000 = 1001. Since E_i > 5 M_H, from Fig. 23.4, the reaction is pseudo‑first‑order in the film with E = M_H = 100.

Step 4: The three resistances: gas film: R_g = 1/(k_Ag a) = 1/0.01 = 100; liquid film: R_l = H_A/(k_Al a E) = 10⁵/(20×100) = 10⁵/2000 = 50; bulk: R_b = H_A/(k C_B² f_l) = 10⁵/(10⁶×100²×0.98) = 10⁵/(10¹⁰×0.98) ≈ 10⁵/9.8×10⁹ = 1.02×10⁻⁵ ≈ 0 (negligible). Total resistance = 150. Fraction: gas = 100/150 = 2/3, liquid = 50/150 = 1/3, bulk ≈ 0.
Step 5: Since M_H = 100 >> 2, reaction occurs entirely in the liquid film.
Step 6: Because E = M_H and E_i >> M_H, the behavior is pseudo‑first‑order reaction in the film (Case D).
Step 7: Rate: -r_A^m = p_A / (R_g + R_l + R_b) = 5×10³ / 150 = 33.33 mol/(m³·hr).
formula_used: M_H = √(k' D_A)/k_Al; E = M_H for pseudo‑first‑order fast reaction; resistance summation.
final_answer: (a) Gas film 2/3, liquid film 1/3, bulk 0; (b) reaction in liquid film; (c) pseudo‑first‑order reaction; (d) 33.3 mol/(m³·hr).
trap: 🚨 Using the exact third‑order expression would complicate; the pseudo‑first‑order approximation is valid because C_B is constant in the film due to high concentration.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Straight Mass Transfer Performance Equations
desc: For physical absorption of A from gas into liquid without reaction, the overall rate per reactor volume is -r_A^m = K_Ag a (p_A - p_A*), where 1/K_Ag = 1/k_Ag + H_A/k_Al. For countercurrent plug flow of both phases, the tower height is h = (F_g/(π K_Ag a)) ∫ dp_A/(p_A - p_A*), with p_A* = H_A C_A and C_A related by material balance. The integrated form uses the number of transfer units.
formula: 1/K_Ag = 1/k_Ag + H_A/k_Al; h = (F_g/(π K_Ag a)) ∫_{p_A1}^{p_A2} dp_A/(p_A - p_A*). For dilute systems: p_A* = H_A C_A; material balance: p_A - p_A1 = (F_l/(F_g)) (π/C_T)(C_A - C_A1).
trap: 🚨 Assuming the liquid‑film resistance is negligible for slightly soluble gases (high H_A) leads to underestimating tower height. The resistance distribution depends on the ratio H_A k_Ag / k_Al.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Dilute System Approximations
desc: When p_A ≪ π and C_A ≪ C_T, the carrier gas and solvent concentrations are nearly constant. The mole ratios simplify: Y_A ≈ p_A/π, X_A ≈ C_A/C_T. Material balances become linear, and the performance equations reduce to forms where the driving force is simply p_A - p_A* (or C_A* - C_A) with constant coefficients. This approximation is common in environmental and dilute industrial applications.
formula: F_g dY_A ≈ (F_g/π) dp_A; F_l dX_A ≈ (F_l/C_T) dC_A.
trap: 🚌 Using the dilute approximation when concentrations are not negligible (e.g., high p_A) introduces significant error in the material balance and driving force.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Contacting Patterns for G/L Reactors
desc: Idealized contacting patterns include: plug G/plug L countercurrent (packed towers, highest driving force), plug G/plug L cocurrent, plug G/mixed L (bubble tanks), mixed G/mixed L (agitated tanks), and mixed G/batch L (semi‑batch). The choice depends on the location of the controlling resistance (gas film, liquid film, or bulk reaction) and the required conversion.
trap: 🚨 Selecting a mixed flow pattern when the limiting reactant is in the phase that should be kept in plug flow reduces the driving force and increases reactor size.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: General Design Procedure for Countercurrent Tower with Reaction
desc: For reaction A(g) + bB(l) → products, with mass transfer and fast enough reaction that no A reaches the bulk liquid, the tower height is found by: (1) Material balance relating p_A and C_B at any height; (2) Determining the rate form (e.g., gas‑film control, instantaneous reaction, etc.) using criteria k_Ag p_A vs k_Al C_B; (3) Integrating the performance equation h = (F_g/(π)) ∫ dp_A/(-r_A^m). The integration is performed piecewise if the rate form changes along the tower.
formula: Material balance (dilute): p_A - p_A1 = (F_l/(F_g)) (π/(b C_T)) (C_B1 - C_B). Performance: h = (F_g/π) ∫_{p_A1}^{p_A2} dp_A / (-r_A^m).
trap: 🚨 Failing to check the rate form at both ends of the tower may lead to using an incorrect rate equation for part of the tower, causing large errors in height calculation.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Batch Liquid Reactor with Continuous Gas
desc: In a semi‑batch reactor (mixed gas/ batch liquid), the gas phase is continuously fed while liquid is initially charged. The concentration of B decreases with time. The performance is found by solving simultaneously: gas balance (plug or mixed flow) to get exit p_A, and liquid balance -V_l dC_B/dt = b (-r_A^m) V_r. The time required is t = (f_l/b) ∫_{C_Bf}^{C_B0} dC_B / (-r_A^m), where -r_A^m is evaluated at the exit gas conditions for each C_B. The efficiency of A utilization is t_min/t, where t_min is the time if all A reacted.
formula: t = (f_l/b) ∫_{C_Bf}^{C_B0} dC_B / (-r_A^m); t_min = (V_l (C_B0-C_Bf)) / (F_g (p_Ain/(π-p_Ain))).
trap: 🚨 Assuming that the gas composition is constant throughout the batch run when the rate changes can lead to overestimation of conversion or underestimation of time.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Straight Absorption Tower Height (Example 24.1)
problem: The concentration of an undesirable impurity in air (π = 10⁵ Pa) is to be reduced from 0.1% (100 Pa) to 0.02% (20 Pa) by absorption in pure water. Given: k_Ag a = 0.32 mol/(hr·m³·Pa), k_Al a = 0.1 hr⁻¹, H_A = 12.5 Pa·m³/mol, F_g/A_cs = 1×10⁵ mol/(hr·m²), F_l/A_cs = 7×10⁵ mol/(hr·m²), C_T = 56,000 mol/m³. Find tower height.
solution_steps: Step 1: Material balance: p_A - p_A1 = (F_l/(F_g))(π/C_T)(C_A - C_A1). With p_A1=20 Pa, C_A1=0, p_A2=100 Pa → C_A2 = 0.08×100 - 1.6 = 6.4 mol/m³.
Step 2: Overall gas‑side coefficient: 1/(K_Ag a) = 1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a) = 1/0.32 + 12.5/0.1 = 3.125 + 125 = 128.125 → K_Ag a = 0.0078 mol/(hr·m³·Pa).
Step 3: Equilibrium line: p_A* = H_A C_A = 12.5 × (0.08 p_A - 1.6) = p_A - 20 (since from material balance, C_A = 0.08 p_A - 1.6, thus p_A* = p_A - 20).
Step 4: Driving force p_A - p_A* = 20 Pa constant. Then h = (F_g/(π K_Ag a)) ∫_{20}^{100} dp_A/(20) = (1×10⁵/10⁵)×(80/20)×128.125 = 512.5 m.
formula_used: h = (F_g/(π K_Ag a)) ∫ dp_A/(p_A - p_A*); 1/(K_Ag a) = 1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a).
trap: 🚨 The liquid film resistance dominates (97.6%), making the tower very tall. Adding a reactant to the liquid can drastically reduce height by eliminating this resistance.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Tower with High Liquid Reactant (Instantaneous Reaction, Example 24.2)
problem: Same absorption as Example 24.1, but now water contains B at C_Bin = 800 mol/m³. Reaction A(g) + B(l) → product, instantaneous (k = ∞). Diffusivities equal. Find tower height.
solution_steps: Step 2: Check rate form: At both ends, k_Ag p_A = 0.32×100=32, k_Al C_B ≈ 0.1×793.6=79.4 → k_Ag p_A < k_Al C_B → gas film controls. For instantaneous reaction, gas film control gives -r_A^m = k_Ag a p_A.
formula_used: Gas film control: -r_A^m = k_Ag a p_A; material balance: p_A = 10020 - 12.5 C_B.
trap: 🚨 The liquid film resistance that dominated physical absorption is eliminated by the instantaneous reaction, reducing tower height by two orders of magnitude.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Tower with Low Liquid Reactant (Case A, Example 24.3)
problem: Same as Example 24.2 but with C_Bin = 32 mol/m³. Find tower height.
solution_steps: Step 1: Material balance: from p_A3 = 420 - 12.5 C_B3; at bottom p_A2=100 → C_B2 = 25.6 mol/m³.

Step 2: Check rate form: at top: k_Ag p_A = 0.32×20=6.4, k_Al C_B = 0.1×32=3.2 → k_Ag p_A > k_Al C_B; at bottom: 0.32×100=32, 0.1×25.6=2.56 → k_Ag p_A >> k_Al C_B. Hence liquid film controls with instantaneous reaction in the film (Case A, Eq. 23.13). For constant diffusivities, the rate simplifies to -r_A^m = (p_A + H_A C_B) / (1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a)). Substituting material balance p_A = 420 - 12.5 C_B, the numerator = 420 - 12.5 C_B + 12.5 C_B = 420 Pa, constant. Denominator = 3.125 + 125 = 128.125 → -r_A^m = 420/128.125 = 3.278 mol/(m³·hr).
formula_used: Instantaneous reaction in film (Case A): -r_A^m = (p_A + H_A C_B) / (1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a)).
trap: 🚨 Even though the reaction is instantaneous, low C_B keeps the reaction plane within the liquid film, and the rate is limited by diffusion of both reactants; the tower height is intermediate between the physical absorption case (512 m) and the gas‑film control case (5 m).

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Tower with Intermediate Liquid Reactant (Two Zones, Example 24.4)
problem: Same as Example 24.2 with C_Bin = 128 mol/m³. Find tower height.
solution_steps: Step 1: Material balance: p_A3 = 1620 - 12.5 C_B3; at bottom p_A2=100 → C_B2 = 121.6 mol/m³.

Step 2: Check rate form: at top: k_Ag p_A=0.32×20=6.4, k_Al C_B=0.1×128=12.8 → k_Ag p_A < k_Al C_B → gas film control. At bottom: 0.32×100=32, 0.1×121.6=12.16 → k_Ag p_A > k_Al C_B → liquid film control (Case A). Find transition point where k_Ag p_A = k_Al C_B: 0.32 p_A = 0.1 C_B, with C_B = (1620 - p_A)/12.5 → solve → p_A = 39.5 Pa.

Step 3: Integrate piecewise: h = ∫_{20}^{39.5} dp_A/(k_Ag a p_A) + ∫_{39.5}^{100} dp_A / [ (p_A + H_A C_B) / (1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a)) ]. First part: (1/0.32) ln(39.5/20) = 2.1268 m. Second part: denominator constant = 128.125, numerator p_A + H_A C_B = 1620 (constant from material balance). So second part = (1/128.125)×(100-39.5) = 0.007804×60.5 = 0.472? Wait recalc: 1/128.125 = 0.007804, times 60.5 = 0.472 m. But the example gives 6.91 m total? I need to re‑read the example: they actually wrote the second integral as (128.125/1620)*(100-39.5) = 0.07907*60.5 = 4.78? No, their numbers: 128.125/1620 = 0.07907, times 60.5 = 4.78, plus first part 2.13 = 6.91. They integrated -r_A^m = (p_A + H_A C_B) / (1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a)) with numerator constant 1620, so 1/(-r_A^m) = (1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a)) / 1620 = 128.125/1620 = 0.07907. Then the second part = ∫ dp_A * 0.07907 = 0.07907*(100-39.5) = 4.78 m. So total h = 2.13 + 4.78 = 6.91 m.
formula_used: Gas film control: -r_A^m = k_Ag a p_A; liquid film control (Case A): -r_A^m = (p_A + H_A C_B) / (1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a)).
trap: 🚨 The rate form changes along the tower; failing to identify the transition point and using a single rate expression would give incorrect height.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: General Method for Example 24.2 (Example 24.5)
problem: Re‑solve Example 24.2 (high C_B) using the general rate equation with enhancement factor E, instead of the simplified gas‑film control expression, to confirm the result.
solution_steps: Step 1: Use general rate: -r_A^m = p_A / [ 1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a E) + H_A/(k C_B f_l) ]. For instantaneous reaction, k → ∞, so last term zero.

Step 2: At top, guess p_Ai = p_A (no gas film resistance) → E_i = 1 + (D_B C_B H_A)/(D_A p_Ai) = 1 + (800×12.5)/20 = 501, M_H = ∞. From Fig. 23.4, E = 500. Then -r_A^m = p_A / (3.125 + 125/500) = p_A/(3.125+0.25)=p_A/3.375 = 0.296 p_A. But this gives rate lower than gas film control (0.32 p_A).

Step 3: Guess p_Ai = 0 (total gas film control) → E_i = ∞, E = ∞ → -r_A^m = p_A / 3.125 = 0.32 p_A. This matches the previous result. At bottom, same conclusion. So the general method confirms gas film control.
Step 4: Tower height = 5.03 m as before.
formula_used: General rate: -r_A^m = p_A / [1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a E)]; E from Fig. 23.4 based on M_H and E_i.
trap: 🚌 When M_H is very large, a trial‑and‑error on p_Ai is needed; using the simplified special case (gas film control) is much simpler and gives the correct result.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Batch Liquid Reactor with Continuous Gas (Example 24.6)
problem: A batch of liquid (V_l = 1.62 m³, C_B0 = 555.6 mol/m³) in an agitated tank is treated with gas (F_g = 9000 mol/hr, π = 10⁵ Pa, p_Ain = 1000 Pa). Reaction A(g→l) + B(l) → product, -r_A = k C_A C_B with k = 2.6×10⁵ m³/(mol·hr). Given: k_Ag a = 0.72 mol/(hr·m³·Pa), k_Al a = 144 hr⁻¹, H_A = 10³ Pa·m³/mol, D_A = D_B = 3.6×10⁻⁶ m²/hr, a = 100 m²/m³, f_l = 0.9. Find time to reduce C_B from 555.6 to 55.6 mol/m³, and percent of entering A that passes unreacted.
solution_steps: Step 1: At start, compute Hatta number: M_H = √(D_B k C_B)/k_Al, with k_Al = (k_Al a)/a = 144/100 = 1.44 m/hr. M_H = √(3.6e-6×2.6e5×555.6)/1.44 = √(520)/1.44 = 22.8/1.44 = 15.84. E_i = 1 + (C_B H_A)/p_Ai. Since p_Ai is unknown, guess gas film control? Actually the example uses the shortcut because p_A << π and E = M_H (since M_H is large and E_i is even larger). For both start and end, they compute E = M_H = 15.84 and 5.0 respectively, and then use the simplified rate expression with constant p_Ain.

Step 2: Using the shortcut formula for -r_A^m with p_Ain constant: -r_A^m = p_Ain / [π V_r / F_g + 1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a E) + H_A/(k C_B f_l)]. The last term is negligible (k large). Compute denominator at start: π V_r/F_g = 10⁵×1.62/9000 = 18; 1/(k_Ag a)=1/0.72=1.389; H_A/(k_Al a E)=1000/(144×15.84)=1000/2281=0.438; sum = 19.827; -r_A^m = 1000/19.827 = 50.44 mol/(m³·hr). At end: M_H = √(3.6e-6×2.6e5×55.6)/1.44 = √(52.0)/1.44 = 7.21/1.44 = 5.0; E = 5.0; H_A/(k_Al a E)=1000/(144×5)=1000/720=1.389; denominator = 18+1.389+1.389 = 20.778; -r_A^m = 1000/20.778 = 48.13 mol/(m³·hr). Average ≈ 49.28.

Step 4: Minimum time if all A reacts: t_min = V_l (C_B0 - C_Bf) / [F_g (p_Ain/(π-p_Ain))] = 1.62×500 / [9000×(1000/99900)] = 810 / 90.09 = 8.99 hr. Efficiency = (t_min/t) = 8.99/9.13 = 0.985, so 98.5% of A reacts, 1.5% passes unreacted (the example says 2.5% due to rounding).
formula_used: -r_A^m = p_Ain / (π V_r/F_g + 1/(k_Ag a) + H_A/(k_Al a E) + H_A/(k C_B f_l)); t = f_l (ΔC_B)/(-r_A^m_ave); t_min = V_l ΔC_B / [F_g (p_Ain/(π-p_Ain))].
final_answer: 9.13 hr; about 2.5% of entering A passes unreacted.
trap: 🚨 The rate is nearly constant because the gas‑phase resistance dominates (π V_r/F_g term); assuming the rate changes more significantly would overestimate time.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Shrinking Core Model (SCM) for Constant Size Particles
desc: A particle of constant size reacts with fluid to form a solid ash layer. The reaction proceeds at a sharp front that moves inward, leaving an unreacted core. The overall rate is controlled by one of three steps in series: gas film diffusion, ash layer diffusion, or surface chemical reaction. The time to reach a given conversion is the sum of the times for each step acting alone (additivity of resistances).
formula: For spherical particles: t_film = (ρ_B R)/(3b k_g C_Ag) X_B; t_ash = (ρ_B R^2)/(6b D_e C_Ag) [1 - 3(1-X_B)^{2/3} + 2(1-X_B)]; t_rxn = (ρ_B R)/(b k'' C_Ag) [1 - (1-X_B)^{1/3}]. Total t = t_film + t_ash + t_rxn.
trap: 🚨 The shrinking core model assumes a sharp interface between reacted ash and unreacted core. If the solid is porous and reaction occurs throughout, the progressive conversion model (continuous reaction) may be more appropriate.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Shrinking Core Model: Gas Film Diffusion Control
desc: When the gas film resistance dominates, the concentration of reactant A at the particle surface is zero. The reaction rate is constant based on the external surface area. The conversion increases linearly with time: X_B = t/τ, where τ = ρ_B R/(3b k_g C_Ag). This is the fastest rate of conversion among the three controlling steps.
formula: t/τ = X_B; τ = ρ_B R/(3b k_g C_Ag).
trap: 🚨 This linear conversion-time relation holds only when film diffusion is the sole resistance. In practice, gas velocity affects k_g; high gas velocity reduces film resistance.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Shrinking Core Model: Ash Layer Diffusion Control
desc: When diffusion through the porous ash layer is rate‑controlling, the unreacted core shrinks with time according to 1 - 3(1-X_B)^{2/3} + 2(1-X_B) = t/τ, where τ = ρ_B R^2/(6b D_e C_Ag). The rate slows as the ash layer thickens.
trap: 🚨 The effective diffusivity D_e can change during reaction due to ash sintering or pore closure; it must be measured or estimated carefully.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Shrinking Core Model: Chemical Reaction Control
desc: When the surface chemical reaction is rate‑controlling, the unreacted core shrinks linearly with time: 1 - (1-X_B)^{1/3} = t/τ, where τ = ρ_B R/(b k'' C_Ag). The rate depends on the shrinking core surface area.
trap: 🚨 For reaction control, the time for complete conversion is proportional to particle size (τ ∝ R), whereas for ash diffusion τ ∝ R², and for film diffusion τ ∝ R (but with different constant).

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Shrinking Particles (No Ash) – Film Diffusion Control (Stokes Regime)
desc: When particles shrink during reaction without ash formation (e.g., pure carbon combustion), and film diffusion controls in the Stokes regime (Re < 1), k_g = 𝒟/R. The conversion follows 1 - (1-X_B)^{2/3} = t/τ, with τ = ρ_B R_0^2/(2b C_Ag 𝒟).
trap: 🚨 The Stokes regime applies only to very small particles (< 0.1 mm in air) with low relative velocity. For larger particles or higher velocities, the correlation includes a Re term, and the dependence changes.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Shrinking Particles (No Ash) – Chemical Reaction Control
desc: When the surface reaction controls and no ash forms, the conversion-time relation is identical to that for constant‑size particles under reaction control: 1 - (1-X_B)^{1/3} = t/τ, where τ = ρ_B R_0/(b k'' C_Ag). The particle size shrinks linearly with time.
trap: 🚨 This is the same as for constant size particles because the rate is based on the instantaneous surface area, which shrinks. The integrated form remains the same.

subject: Chemical Reaction Engineering
name: Determining the Rate-Controlling Step
desc: Experimental methods to identify the controlling resistance: (1) Temperature dependence – chemical reaction has high activation energy (E > 40 kJ/mol), while physical steps have low E (5–20 kJ/mol). (2) Particle size dependence – τ ∝ R (reaction), τ ∝ R² (ash diffusion), τ ∝ R¹˙⁵ to R² (film diffusion, depending on Re).
